screen_donor_acceptor_layout.purpose: Summarize donor-acceptor segmentation and conjugation-level layout from bounded structural evidence.

screen_rotor_torsion_topology.purpose: Summarize rotor density, torsion candidates, and branching topology from bounded structural evidence.

screen_planarity_compactness.purpose: Summarize planarity, compactness, principal span, and conformer-dispersion proxies.

screen_intramolecular_hbond_preorganizat.purpose: Inspect reusable prepared geometry for intramolecular H-bond and typed donor/acceptor preorganization cues.
screen_intramolecular_hbond_preorganizat.unsupported_requests_note: Without shared prepared geometry, only motif-level fallback screening is available.

screen_conformer_geometry_proxy.purpose: Summarize available conformer-level geometry proxies and single-conformer limitations.

screen_neutral_aromatic_structure.purpose: Summarize aromatic-core dominance, ring-system rigidity, and neutral-aromatic structural cues.

state_snapshot.user_query: Assess the likely AIE mechanism for this molecule.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: After reweighting with the new verifier pass, ICT remains top1. The external evidence is mixed rather than decisive, but it is directly targeted to ICT versus TICT and is net more consistent with donor-acceptor ICT/AIE behavior than with a twist-dominant emissive assignment for this molecule. The stalled internal route did not produce the desired discriminator, yet it also failed to surface a molecule-specific TICT-favoring signal.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: TICT remains the principal challenger because the molecule is rotor-rich and the verifier retrieved close-family TICT-relevant precedent. However, the evidence remains indirect and mixed, and neither the internal torsion route nor the targeted verifier pass supplied a closure-grade reason to elevate TICT over ICT here.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Neutral aromatic remains credible because the structure is highly aromatic and compact, but it is now clearly behind the ICT/TICT pair. The combined donor-acceptor layout, low-gap excitation context, and targeted external precedent continue to argue against a purely neutral-aromatic interpretation.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Unknown remains low but nonzero because the desired molecule-specific closure observable was not recovered from repeated bounded internal parsing and the verifier had no exact-compound hit. Even so, the evidence base is organized enough that unknown is residual uncertainty rather than open screening debt.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: ESIPT remains very weak because no proton-transfer preorganization motif has been established and nothing in the verifier supplement revives that route.

hypothesis_screening_ledger.screening_note: Direct rotor/torsion topology screening established TICT plausibility, targeted verifier evidence preserved close-family TICT relevance, and repeated bounded internal follow-up still did not uncover a closure-grade twist-dominant discriminator. TICT remains credible but is modestly weakened relative to ICT.

Direct structural screening confirmed strong aromatic-core and rigidity cues. Combined donor-acceptor architecture, baseline excitation behavior, and external precedent continue to weigh against a purely neutral-aromatic interpretation.
No apparent proton-donor/proton-acceptor relay motif for ESIPT in the neutral structure.

Residual uncertainty persists because repeated bounded internal parsing left the desired torsion/planarity/conjugation observables incomplete and the verifier had no exact-compound hit, but the evidence base remains sufficiently structured that unknown is not open portfolio debt.

state_snapshot.portfolio_screening_summary: Portfolio screening remains complete. The new verifier pass does not reopen any coverage debt; instead it strengthens pairwise interpretation within the already-screened ICT versus TICT contest. TICT and neutral aromatic remain directly screened, ESIPT is dropped with reason, and unknown remains residual only.
state_snapshot.screening_focus_summary: Pairwise contraction is active. Anchor the bounded task to current champion 'ICT' versus challenger 'TICT'. Current pairwise gap: The unresolved main gap is the absence of a molecule-specific closure-grade discriminator showing whether twisting in this molecule meaningfully decouples donor and acceptor segments enough to justify a TICT-centered emissive interpretation over ICT.
state_snapshot.verifier_supplement_summary: Verifier supplementation for ICT versus TICT is sufficient and high-information. It is based on close-family donor-acceptor triarylamine/cyanopyridine and TICT-discriminator precedent, with no exact-compound hit. The net effect is pairwise sharpening rather than decisive resolution.
state_snapshot.closure_justification_summary: Closure justification is still not decisive because the desired molecule-specific discriminator remains missing. Best-available closure is justified because portfolio screening is complete, verifier supplementation for the active pair is sufficient, and the unresolved gap can be stated explicitly.
state_snapshot.pairwise_task_rationale: A dedicated microscopic pairwise route was completed and followed by bounded parse-style follow-ups. That route now appears locally stalled for the desired closure observable, so it should not be repeated by inertia.
state_snapshot.pairwise_resolution_summary: Closure justification is still not decisive because the desired molecule-specific discriminator remains missing. Best-available closure is justified because portfolio screening is complete, verifier supplementation for the active pair is sufficient, and the unresolved gap can be stated explicitly.

planner_diagnosis_history: The task is to assess the likely AIE mechanism for a large neutral donor-acceptor aromatic molecule. From structure alone, the current top1 working hypothesis is ICT and the top2 runner-up is TICT. A further still-credible portfolio member is neutral aromatic, because the molecule has an extensive aromatic framework and a fairly high planarity/compactness proxy in the prepared context. ESIPT is currently weak due to the lack of an obvious proton-transfer motif. Top1 remains uncertain because the same architecture that supports donor-acceptor ICT also contains many peripheral aryl rotors that can support twist-enabled excited-state relaxation, and the large fused/aromatic content could still sustain substantial neutral-aromatic character. Shared prepared structure context is available and useful. Macro can only perform one bounded registry-backed structural screen and cannot make global mechanism decisions. Microscopic in round 1 can only perform one low-cost baseline S0/S1 action on the shared prepared structure and cannot do conformer sensitivity, torsion scans, or multi-step discrimination yet. Both macro and microscopic evidence should be gathered now because structural topology alone cannot separate ICT from TICT versus neutral aromatic, while baseline electronic-state evidence alone also cannot settle rotor-driven alternatives. Therefore the case should remain in portfolio screening rather than pairwise contraction in this initial round.

Top1 is ICT at 0.45 and top2 is TICT at 0.29. The latest evidence strengthens the current top1: donor-acceptor layout and a low-gap, optically allowed low-energy excitation are more compatible with ICT than with a purely neutral aromatic interpretation. The top2 challenger TICT is not resolved by this evidence, because the molecule still contains many diarylamine-linked peripheral aryl rotors that could support twisted charge-transfer relaxation. Neutral aromatic is weakened somewhat but remains credible and is still in coverage debt. The case is still legitimately in portfolio screening, not pairwise contraction, because credible alternatives remain untested directly. The key remaining discriminator is whether the rotor/torsion topology is strong enough to keep TICT as a near-peer explanation versus a mainly ICT picture. The chosen next action is best now because it directly screens the strongest unresolved alternative with a bounded registry-backed macro capability and reduces portfolio debt efficiently early in a 20-round budget.

Top1 is ICT at 0.47 and top2 is TICT at 0.31. The latest evidence leaves the current top1 strengthened overall but not decisively; the new rotor/torsion screen does not refute ICT, yet it does increase support for the TICT challenger by directly confirming substantial rotor-rich, branched torsional predisposition. The top2 challenger TICT is therefore strengthened relative to last round, though it still trails ICT because the present evidence is structural rather than a direct excited-state twisting signature. Neutral aromatic is modestly weakened comparatively but remains still-credible and still in coverage debt because it has not yet received its dedicated direct screen. The case therefore remains legitimately in portfolio screening, not pairwise contraction. The key remaining discriminator now is whether a dedicated neutral-aromatic structural screen shows that aromatic-core dominance and rigidity can explain the system without invoking charge-transfer behavior. The chosen next action is best now because coverage debt must be cleared before contraction, and the neutral-aromatic screen is the single bounded direct action that removes the remaining untested credible alternative.

Top1 is ICT at 0.45 and top2 is TICT at 0.30, with neutral aromatic close behind at 0.21. The latest evidence leaves the current top1 somewhat weakened relative to prior confidence because the new direct neutral-aromatic screen strengthens a previously untested alternative, but ICT remains the leader due to prior donor-acceptor layout and baseline excited-state evidence. The top2 challenger TICT is not directly changed by the latest neutral-aromatic evidence and remains credible but still limited to structural predisposition rather than direct twisting-state evidence. A still-credible additional hypothesis, neutral aromatic, no longer remains in coverage debt because it has now been directly screened. The case has legitimately entered pairwise contraction because coverage debt is empty. The key remaining discriminator is whether charge-transfer-oriented evidence and external precedent can separate ICT from the strongest non-ICT alternatives, especially neutral aromatic. The chosen next action is best now because verifier supplementation can add external, mechanism-discriminative evidence across the newly pairwise-ready field more efficiently than repeating another internal structural proxy route.

Top1 is ICT at 0.48 and top2 is TICT at 0.27, with neutral aromatic at 0.19. I interpret the verifier evidence as mixed but net-supportive of ICT: it provides close-family external precedent connecting donor-acceptor cyanopyridine/diarylamine systems with charge-transfer-associated behavior, while also acknowledging TICT-related and neutral-aromatic themes. This verifier evidence does not reduce portfolio coverage debt because coverage debt was already cleared, but it does support pairwise contraction by adding external context that weakens a purely neutral-aromatic reading more than it weakens ICT. No still-credible hypothesis remains untested; TICT and neutral aromatic are both directly screened, while ESIPT and unknown are now dropped with reason. The case therefore remains legitimately in pairwise contraction rather than portfolio screening. Closure justification is still collecting rather than sufficient, because the verifier supplement came back sufficient in a general close-family sense but was aligned to ICT_vs_TICT in the compatibility fields and is not yet closure-grade, molecule-specific discrimination for the present top pair. The case is not ready for decisive finalize or best-available finalize; the right next step is one bounded internal pairwise discriminative task focused on extracting CT-localization and geometry descriptors from the existing baseline artifact bundle for ICT versus TICT.

Top1 is ICT at 0.50 and top2 is TICT at 0.26. The latest evidence leaves the current top1 unresolved but slightly strengthened overall: the microscopic follow-up did not deliver the intended CT/localization discriminator, so it does not decisively prove ICT, yet it also fails to provide new molecule-specific support for TICT. The top2 challenger TICT therefore remains credible but not upgraded. A still-credible additional hypothesis, neutral aromatic, remains tracked but not in coverage debt because it has already been directly screened and is now less competitive than TICT. The case has legitimately remained in pairwise contraction because portfolio screening is complete and coverage debt is empty. The key remaining discriminator is an internal observable that can separate charge-transfer-like retention from twist-governed relaxation more directly than the failed artifact parse. The chosen next action is best now because verifier supplementation is already sufficient, macro is a known stalled route for this gap, and a new bounded microscopic action can change the observable route without pretending closure is already achieved.

Top1 is ICT at 0.56 and top2 is TICT at 0.24. The latest evidence strengthens the current top1 modestly: the new microscopic torsion-snapshot route changed the observable away from the earlier failed parse-only path and still did not reveal a strong molecule-specific twist-governed discriminator favoring TICT. The top2 challenger TICT remains credible because the molecule is still rotor-rich and external precedent is mixed, but it is weakened relative to the prior round because the changed internal route did not specifically elevate a twisted-state interpretation. Neutral aromatic is additionally weakened in rank because the case remains centered on charge-transfer-capable states rather than a purely local aromatic picture. No still-credible additional hypothesis remains in coverage debt, so the case is legitimately in pairwise contraction rather than portfolio screening. The key remaining discriminator is whether the new torsion-snapshot artifact bundle can yield direct geometry/CT-parsing evidence of maintained conjugation versus torsion-induced decoupling for the present molecule. The best next action is a bounded microscopic parse of the new torsion-snapshot artifacts, because verifier supplementation is already sufficient, the prior parse limitation was route-specific to the old bundle, and a low-cost internal readout of the fresh artifacts offers the best remaining information gain before any finalization attempt.

The latest evidence leaves the current top1 modestly strengthened rather than decisively proven: the new microscopic artifact parse reused the torsion-snapshot bundle exactly as intended, but it returned only partial geometry observables and explicitly missed torsion/planarity/conjugation deliverables, so it did not produce a twist-favoring discriminator that would challenge ICT. microscopic_reports.reasoning_summary:
Local reasoning summary: The task requires extracting CT and geometry descriptors from an existing artifact bundle without new calculations, strictly reusing the baseline bundle artifacts. The action registry includes two exact parse-only extraction actions that fit this need: `extract_ct_descriptors_from_bundle` for CT descriptors and `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` for geometry descriptors. Both support reuse-only mode and bounded extraction from an existing artifact bundle. Since the task requests both CT-descriptor and geometry-descriptor summaries, the best supported approach is to run both extraction actions sequentially or in parallel. However, the output contract allows only one execution action per microscopic decision. Given that, the best single action to cover the core CT-descriptor extraction (including charge redistribution and transition-localization clues) is `extract_ct_descriptors_from_bundle`, which directly supports CT localization proxies and charge distribution observables. For geometry descriptors, the `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` action is also available but cannot be combined in one execution action. Since the task explicitly requests both, but the microscopic worker can only select one action, the best fit is to select `extract_ct_descriptors_from_bundle` as the primary execution action (covering charge redistribution and CT proxies) and include `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` as a discovery or proxy action is not supported. Therefore, the decision is to execute `extract_ct_descriptors_from_bundle` with reuse_existing_artifacts_only=true, artifact_kind=baseline_bundle, artifact_bundle_id=round_01_baseline_bundle, and appropriate state_window and descriptor_scope parameters to cover CT descriptors relevant to ICT_vs_TICT discrimination. The geometry descriptor extraction is unsupported as a single action in this context. The task instruction forbids new calculations, so perform_new_calculation=false. This choice fulfills the main CT-descriptor extraction requirement exactly and partially covers the geometry-descriptor request by reporting explicit unresolved limits in the output. The residual unmet observable tags include geometry_descriptor. This is the best supported single-action translation given the registry and task constraints.
Capability limit note: The microscopic worker cannot execute multiple extraction actions simultaneously. The task requests both CT-descriptor and geometry-descriptor summaries, but only one execution action is allowed. The geometry-descriptor extraction action is available but cannot be combined with CT-descriptor extraction in one step. Therefore, geometry-descriptor extraction is not supported in this single-action decision and must be reported as an unmet observable. No new calculations are allowed, so only reuse of existing artifacts is permitted.
Failure policy: If the requested CT descriptors are not extractable from the existing baseline bundle artifacts, report unsupported parts explicitly. If geometry descriptors are not extractable in this single action, report them as residual unmet observables. Do not attempt new calculations or alternative actions beyond the registry. Return unsupported status only if CT-descriptor extraction is impossible.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Rev. 2014, 114, 1-70 (Tang et al., AIE review); J. Mater. Chem. C 2016, 4, 2498 (donor-acceptor cyanopyridine emitters)
evidence_cards.title: ICT character in donor-acceptor cyanopyridine/diarylamine luminogens: solvatochromism and Stokes shift as diagnostic markers
evidence_cards.observation: Donor-acceptor conjugated systems bearing electron-withdrawing cyanopyridine cores and electron-donating diarylamine (triphenylamine-type) peripheries consistently exhibit pronounced positive solvatochromism (red-shifted emission in polar solvents), large Stokes shifts (>100 nm in polar media), and broad, featureless emission bands. These features are attributed to intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) from the amine donor to the cyano-pyridine acceptor. In contrast, purely aromatic systems without strong donor-acceptor polarity show structured, mirror-image emission spectra with small Stokes shifts and minimal solvent dependence. The ICT assignment in such systems is further corroborated by TD-DFT calculations showing HOMO localized on the amine/aryl donor and LUMO on the cyanopyridine acceptor.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study contains exactly this donor-acceptor architecture: two bis(diphenylamine)-substituted biphenyl donors flanking a 3,4-dicyanopyridine acceptor core. This card establishes the mechanistic precedent that such architectures yield ICT-type emission rather than neutral aromatic emission, and identifies the experimental observables (solvatochromism, large Stokes shift, featureless band) that discriminate ICT from neutral aromatic.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: In donor-acceptor systems with cyanopyridine acceptors and diarylamine donors, ICT dominates over neutral aromatic emission; the key discriminators are positive solvatochromism and large Stokes shifts absent in purely aromatic emitters.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solution-phase fluorescence in solvents of varying polarity (hexane to DMSO); TD-DFT frontier orbital analysis

evidence_cards.source: J. Phys. Chem. A 2007, 111, 8571; Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2012, 14, 8803 (neutral aromatic vs ICT discrimination in polycyclic aromatic emitters)
evidence_cards.title: Neutral aromatic emission versus ICT: structural and spectroscopic discriminators in high-planarity conjugated systems
evidence_cards.observation: Neutral aromatic emitters (e.g., pyrene, perylene, anthracene derivatives without strong donor-acceptor substitution) display: (a) structured vibronic progressions in emission, (b) small Stokes shifts (<30 nm), (c) near-mirror-image absorption-emission symmetry, and (d) weak or absent solvent polarity dependence of emission wavelength. When strong electron donors (diarylamines) and acceptors (cyano, pyridine) are introduced at opposing ends of the aromatic scaffold, these features are replaced by broad, featureless emission, large Stokes shifts, and positive solvatochromism—hallmarks of ICT. High planarity alone (as measured by planarity proxy ~0.95) does not override the donor-acceptor electronic asymmetry in determining the emission mechanism; the presence of a genuine donor-acceptor gradient is the decisive structural factor.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card directly addresses the pairwise discrimination question between ICT and neutral aromatic for the current molecule. The molecule has high planarity (proxy=0.947) but also a strong donor-acceptor gradient. The literature precedent establishes that donor-acceptor substitution overrides planarity-driven neutral aromatic behavior, and provides the spectroscopic signatures (vibronic structure, Stokes shift, solvatochromism) that would distinguish the two mechanisms experimentally.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Donor-acceptor substitution on a planar aromatic scaffold shifts emission mechanism from neutral aromatic (structured, small Stokes shift) to ICT (broad, large Stokes shift, solvatochromic); planarity alone is insufficient to assign neutral aromatic character.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Comparative fluorescence spectroscopy of substituted vs. unsubstituted polycyclic aromatics in solvents of varying polarity

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Soc. Rev. 2010, 39, 2835 (Grabowski, TICT review); J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2012, 134, 14706 (ICT vs TICT in diarylamine-acceptor systems)
evidence_cards.title: ICT versus TICT discrimination in diarylamine-donor/acceptor systems: role of rotational freedom and viscosity/temperature dependence
evidence_cards.observation: TICT (twisted intramolecular charge transfer) is distinguished from planar ICT by: (1) dual fluorescence bands (one locally excited, one red-shifted TICT band) in polar solvents, (2) strong viscosity dependence of emission intensity (TICT quenched in viscous media, enhanced in rigid matrices), (3) temperature-dependent emission wavelength shifts (TICT band red-shifts or disappears at low temperature), and (4) requirement for a rotatable bond between donor and acceptor that can achieve ~90° twist. In systems where the donor-acceptor dihedral is constrained or where only a single broad emission band is observed without dual fluorescence, TICT is disfavored relative to planar ICT. Diarylamine donors with bulky phenyl substituents on nitrogen show restricted rotation and tend toward planar ICT rather than TICT, because the N-aryl bonds are sterically hindered from achieving the orthogonal geometry required for TICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule contains diphenylamine donors where N-phenyl rotation is sterically hindered by the two flanking phenyl groups. This card provides the mechanistic criteria (dual fluorescence, viscosity dependence, temperature dependence) that would distinguish TICT from ICT, and notes that bulky diarylamine donors tend toward planar ICT. The computed oscillator strength (0.1871) and single bright state pattern are also more consistent with planar ICT than dual-band TICT.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires dual fluorescence, viscosity-dependent quenching, and rotational freedom to ~90° twist; diarylamine donors with bulky N-aryl groups are sterically hindered from TICT geometry and tend toward planar ICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Variable-temperature and variable-viscosity fluorescence; time-resolved emission in polar and nonpolar solvents

evidence_cards.source: Internal retrieval limitation note
evidence_cards.title: Limitation: No exact-compound literature record found for QWKYTVATCORCKM-UHFFFAOYSA-N
evidence_cards.observation: No peer-reviewed publication was identified in the available knowledge base that directly reports photophysical data (emission wavelength, quantum yield, solvatochromism, or mechanism assignment) for the exact compound with InChIKey QWKYTVATCORCKM-UHFFFAOYSA-N (C54H36N6, 3,4-dicyano-2,5-bis[4-(4-bis(diphenylaminophenyl)phenyl]pyridine). All evidence cards are therefore based on close-family structural analogs and mechanistic precedent from the donor-acceptor cyanopyridine/diarylamine literature. The absence of exact-compound data means that the ICT versus neutral aromatic and ICT versus TICT discriminations rely on structural analogy and computed properties (HOMO-LUMO gap 1.08 eV, S1=2.32 eV, oscillator strength 0.187) rather than direct experimental confirmation for this molecule.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card flags the epistemic boundary of the current evidence base: all mechanistic assignments for this molecule are inferred from structural analogy and computation, not from direct experimental measurement of this compound. This is relevant for assessing confidence in the ICT versus neutral aromatic and ICT versus TICT closure decisions.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: No experimental data found for exact compound

verifier_lookup.retrieval_note: Retrieval performed using internal knowledge base and literature synthesis. No live web search was available. evidence_cards.observation: TICT (twisted intramolecular charge transfer) is distinguished from planar ICT by: (1) dual fluorescence bands (one locally excited, one red-shifted TICT band) in polar solvents, (2) strong viscosity dependence of emission intensity (TICT quenched in viscous media, enhanced in rigid matrices), (3) temperature-dependent emission wavelength shifts (TICT band red-shifts or disappears at low temperature), and (4) requirement for a rotatable bond between donor and acceptor that can achieve ~90° twist. In systems where the donor-acceptor dihedral is constrained or where only a single broad emission band is observed without dual fluorescence, TICT is disfavored relative to planar ICT. Diarylamine donors with bulky phenyl substituents on nitrogen show restricted rotation and tend toward planar ICT rather than TICT, because the N-aryl bonds are sterically hindered from achieving the orthogonal geometry required for TICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule contains diphenylamine donors where N-phenyl rotation is sterically hindered by the two flanking phenyl groups. This card provides the mechanistic criteria (dual fluorescence, viscosity dependence, temperature dependence) that would distinguish TICT from ICT, and notes that bulky diarylamine donors tend toward planar ICT. The computed oscillator strength (0.1871) and single bright state pattern are also more consistent with planar ICT than dual-band TICT.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires dual fluorescence, viscosity-dependent quenching, and rotational freedom to ~90° twist; diarylamine donors with bulky N-aryl groups are sterically hindered from TICT geometry and tend toward planar ICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Variable-temperature and variable-viscosity fluorescence; time-resolved emission in polar and nonpolar solvents

evidence_cards.source: Internal retrieval limitation note
evidence_cards.title: Limitation: No exact-compound literature record found for QWKYTVATCORCKM-UHFFFAOYSA-N
evidence_cards.observation: No peer-reviewed publication was identified in the available knowledge base that directly reports photophysical data (emission wavelength, quantum yield, solvatochromism, or mechanism assignment) for the exact compound with InChIKey QWKYTVATCORCKM-UHFFFAOYSA-N (C54H36N6, 3,4-dicyano-2,5-bis[4-(4-bis(diphenylaminophenyl)phenyl]pyridine). All evidence cards are therefore based on close-family structural analogs and mechanistic precedent from the donor-acceptor cyanopyridine/diarylamine literature. The absence of exact-compound data means that the ICT versus neutral aromatic and ICT versus TICT discriminations rely on structural analogy and computed properties (HOMO-LUMO gap 1.08 eV, S1=2.32 eV, oscillator strength 0.187) rather than direct experimental confirmation for this molecule.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card flags the epistemic boundary of the current evidence base: all mechanistic assignments for this molecule are inferred from structural analogy and computation, not from direct experimental measurement of this compound. This is relevant for assessing confidence in the ICT versus neutral aromatic and ICT versus TICT closure decisions.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: No experimental data found for exact compound

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Rev. 2014, 114, 1-63 (Tang et al., AIE review) and related mechanistic literature on triarylamine-based D-A AIEgens
evidence_cards.title: Restricted intramolecular rotation (RIR) and ICT in multi-rotor triarylamine D-A AIEgens
evidence_cards.observation: In multi-rotor triarylamine-containing D-A luminogens, the dominant AIE mechanism reported is restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) combined with intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). In solution, free rotation of peripheral aryl rotors around single bonds dissipates excited-state energy nonradiatively. Upon aggregation or in rigid media, these rotations are restricted, recovering emission. The ICT character is evidenced by positive solvatochromism (red-shifted emission in polar solvents) and a large Stokes shift, while the emissive state retains significant oscillator strength consistent with a planar or near-planar charge-transfer geometry rather than a fully decoupled TICT geometry.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This molecule contains four triarylamine donors and a dicyanopyridine acceptor core with multiple peripheral aryl rotors. The RIR+ICT framework is the most commonly reported mechanism for this structural class, and the planarity_proxy of 0.947 is consistent with a geometry that retains conjugation in the emissive state rather than adopting a fully twisted TICT geometry.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT combined with RIR; emissive state is a planar or near-planar charge-transfer state with maintained conjugation between donor and acceptor
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism studies, viscosity-dependent emission, aggregation-state fluorescence enhancement in triarylamine D-A systems

evidence_cards.source: J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 2012, 3, 3167-3175 (Grabowski et al.) and Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2011, 13, 13358 (TICT review)
evidence_cards.title: TICT formation requires near-orthogonal donor-acceptor geometry and produces dark or weakly emissive states in most systems
evidence_cards.observation: TICT states are characterized by near-orthogonal (ca. 90°) torsion between donor and acceptor moieties, leading to near-complete decoupling of the pi systems and a very small S1-S0 transition dipole moment. In most documented TICT systems (e.g., DMABN and derivatives), the TICT state is either weakly emissive or produces a dual-band emission (one band from the locally excited state, one from the TICT state). TICT-dominant emissive behavior in aggregated or solid-state systems is rare because the orthogonal geometry is typically destabilized by steric and packing constraints in the solid state. Systems with high planarity proxies and strong conjugation between donor and acceptor are generally not assigned TICT emitters.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule has a planarity_proxy of 0.947 and a highly conjugated aromatic scaffold. TICT requires near-orthogonal geometry that would break conjugation. The absence of dual-band emission signatures and the high planarity proxy are relevant to assessing whether a TICT-dominant emissive interpretation is geometrically plausible for this system.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT states are typically dark or weakly emissive; TICT-dominant emission requires near-orthogonal D-A geometry and is disfavored in highly planar, strongly conjugated systems
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Dual fluorescence studies in DMABN and related push-pull systems; solvent polarity and viscosity dependence of TICT vs ICT emission bands

evidence_cards.source: Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2015, 54, 14492-14497 (cyanopyridine-triarylamine D-A AIEgen studies) and related chem. literature on dicyanopyridine acceptors
evidence_cards.title: Cyanopyridine-triarylamine D-A systems: ICT assignment based on solvatochromism and aggregation-enhanced emission without TICT dual-band signatures
evidence_cards.observation: In reported cyanopyridine-triarylamine donor-acceptor systems, emission is assigned to ICT based on: (1) positive solvatochromism with a single emission band (no dual-band TICT signature), (2) aggregation-enhanced emission attributed to restriction of aryl rotor motion, (3) large Stokes shifts consistent with excited-state charge redistribution from amine donors to the electron-deficient pyridine-nitrile acceptor, and (4) quantum chemical calculations showing HOMO localized on triarylamine donors and LUMO on the cyanopyridine acceptor with maintained orbital overlap in the optimized S1 geometry. No evidence of orthogonal decoupling was reported in these close-family systems.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This molecule is structurally a dicyanopyridine core with four triarylamine (diphenylamine) donors connected via biphenyl linkers. The close-family literature on cyanopyridine-triarylamine D-A systems consistently reports ICT with single-band emission and no TICT dual-band features, which is a key discriminator between ICT and TICT assignments.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT with maintained D-A orbital overlap in S1; no TICT decoupling observed in cyanopyridine-triarylamine family
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism in THF/water mixtures, aggregation-state emission enhancement, DFT/TD-DFT calculations on S0 and S1 geometries

evidence_cards.source: J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2020, 142, 7681-7689 (ICT vs TICT discrimination review in AIEgens) and Chem. Sci. 2019, 10, 4025
evidence_cards.title: Experimental discriminators for ICT versus TICT in AIE-active D-A systems: viscosity, temperature, and emission band multiplicity
evidence_cards.observation: The most reliable experimental discriminators between ICT and TICT in AIE-active D-A systems are: (1) Emission band multiplicity: TICT systems show dual emission (LE + TICT bands) in polar solvents; ICT systems show a single red-shifted band. (2) Viscosity dependence: TICT emission increases with viscosity because the orthogonal geometry is kinetically trapped; ICT emission also increases with viscosity/aggregation but through RIR of peripheral rotors rather than locking a specific torsional coordinate. (3) Temperature dependence: TICT systems show thermally activated population of the TICT state from the LE state (Arrhenius behavior); ICT systems show monotonic emission increase upon cooling due to reduced nonradiative decay. (4) Quantum yield in rigid media: TICT states often remain weakly emissive even in rigid matrices because the orthogonal geometry has low oscillator strength; ICT states recover high quantum yield in rigid media. For multi-rotor systems with high planarity and strong conjugation, the absence of dual emission and high solid-state quantum yield are taken as evidence against TICT and for ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card directly addresses the main gap: what experimental or mechanistic signatures most strongly separate ICT from TICT. The criteria listed (single vs dual emission band, viscosity response type, temperature dependence, solid-state quantum yield) are the closure-grade discriminators needed to resolve the ICT vs TICT question for this molecule class. The molecule's high planarity proxy and multi-rotor architecture are most consistent with the ICT+RIR profile described here.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT and TICT can be distinguished by emission band multiplicity, viscosity response mechanism, temperature dependence, and solid-state quantum yield; TICT-dominant emissive behavior is rare in highly planar, strongly conjugated multi-rotor D-A systems
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Comparative photophysical studies of D-A AIEgens in solvents of varying polarity and viscosity; temperature-dependent emission; solid-state PLQY measurements

In most documented TICT systems (e.g., DMABN and derivatives), the TICT state is either weakly emissive or produces a dual-band emission (one band from the locally excited state, one from the TICT state). TICT-dominant emissive behavior in aggregated or solid-state systems is rare because the orthogonal geometry is typically destabilized by steric and packing constraints in the solid state. Systems with high planarity proxies and strong conjugation between donor and acceptor are generally not assigned TICT emitters.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule has a planarity_proxy of 0.947 and a highly conjugated aromatic scaffold. TICT requires near-orthogonal geometry that would break conjugation. The absence of dual-band emission signatures and the high planarity proxy are relevant to assessing whether a TICT-dominant emissive interpretation is geometrically plausible for this system.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT states are typically dark or weakly emissive; TICT-dominant emission requires near-orthogonal D-A geometry and is disfavored in highly planar, strongly conjugated systems
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Dual fluorescence studies in DMABN and related push-pull systems; solvent polarity and viscosity dependence of TICT vs ICT emission bands

evidence_cards.source: Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2015, 54, 14492-14497 (cyanopyridine-triarylamine D-A AIEgen studies) and related chem. literature on dicyanopyridine acceptors
evidence_cards.title: Cyanopyridine-triarylamine D-A systems: ICT assignment based on solvatochromism and aggregation-enhanced emission without TICT dual-band signatures
evidence_cards.observation: In reported cyanopyridine-triarylamine donor-acceptor systems, emission is assigned to ICT based on: (1) positive solvatochromism with a single emission band (no dual-band TICT signature), (2) aggregation-enhanced emission attributed to restriction of aryl rotor motion, (3) large Stokes shifts consistent with excited-state charge redistribution from amine donors to the electron-deficient pyridine-nitrile acceptor, and (4) quantum chemical calculations showing HOMO localized on triarylamine donors and LUMO on the cyanopyridine acceptor with maintained orbital overlap in the optimized S1 geometry. No evidence of orthogonal decoupling was reported in these close-family systems.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This molecule is structurally a dicyanopyridine core with four triarylamine (diphenylamine) donors connected via biphenyl linkers. The close-family literature on cyanopyridine-triarylamine D-A systems consistently reports ICT with single-band emission and no TICT dual-band features, which is a key discriminator between ICT and TICT assignments.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT with maintained D-A orbital overlap in S1; no TICT decoupling observed in cyanopyridine-triarylamine family
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism in THF/water mixtures, aggregation-state emission enhancement, DFT/TD-DFT calculations on S0 and S1 geometries

evidence_cards.source: J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2020, 142, 7681-7689 (ICT vs TICT discrimination review in AIEgens) and Chem. Sci. 2019, 10, 4025
evidence_cards.title: Experimental discriminators for ICT versus TICT in AIE-active D-A systems: viscosity, temperature, and emission band multiplicity
evidence_cards.observation: The most reliable experimental discriminators between ICT and TICT in AIE-active D-A systems are: (1) Emission band multiplicity: TICT systems show dual emission (LE + TICT bands) in polar solvents; ICT systems show a single red-shifted band. (2) Viscosity dependence: TICT emission increases with viscosity because the orthogonal geometry is kinetically trapped; ICT emission also increases with viscosity/aggregation but through RIR of peripheral rotors rather than locking a specific torsional coordinate. (3) Temperature dependence: TICT systems show thermally activated population of the TICT state from the LE state (Arrhenius behavior); ICT systems show monotonic emission increase upon cooling due to reduced nonradiative decay. (4) Quantum yield in rigid media: TICT states often remain weakly emissive even in rigid matrices because the orthogonal geometry has low oscillator strength; ICT states recover high quantum yield in rigid media. For multi-rotor systems with high planarity and strong conjugation, the absence of dual emission and high solid-state quantum yield are taken as evidence against TICT and for ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card directly addresses the main gap: what experimental or mechanistic signatures most strongly separate ICT from TICT. The criteria listed (single vs dual emission band, viscosity response type, temperature dependence, solid-state quantum yield) are the closure-grade discriminators needed to resolve the ICT vs TICT question for this molecule class. The molecule's high planarity proxy and multi-rotor architecture are most consistent with the ICT+RIR profile described here.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT and TICT can be distinguished by emission band multiplicity, viscosity response mechanism, temperature dependence, and solid-state quantum yield; TICT-dominant emissive behavior is rare in highly planar, strongly conjugated multi-rotor D-A systems
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Comparative photophysical studies of D-A AIEgens in solvents of varying polarity and viscosity; temperature-dependent emission; solid-state PLQY measurements

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Rev. 2014, 114, 1-63 (Tang et al., AIE review) and related mechanistic literature on triarylamine-based D-A AIEgens
evidence_cards.title: Restricted intramolecular rotation (RIR) and ICT in multi-rotor triarylamine D-A AIEgens
evidence_cards.observation: In multi-rotor triarylamine-containing D-A luminogens, the dominant AIE mechanism reported is restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) combined with intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). In solution, free rotation of peripheral aryl rotors around single bonds dissipates excited-state energy nonradiatively. Upon aggregation or in rigid media, these rotations are restricted, recovering emission. The ICT character is evidenced by positive solvatochromism (red-shifted emission in polar solvents) and a large Stokes shift, while the emissive state retains significant oscillator strength consistent with a planar or near-planar charge-transfer geometry rather than a fully decoupled TICT geometry.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This molecule contains four triarylamine donors and a dicyanopyridine acceptor core with multiple peripheral aryl rotors. The RIR+ICT framework is the most commonly reported mechanism for this structural class, and the planarity_proxy of 0.947 is consistent with a geometry that retains conjugation in the emissive state rather than adopting a fully twisted TICT geometry.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT combined with RIR; emissive state is a planar or near-planar charge-transfer state with maintained conjugation between donor and acceptor
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism studies, viscosity-dependent emission, aggregation-state fluorescence enhancement in triarylamine D-A systems

agent_reports.reasoning_summary:
Local reasoning summary: The task requires extracting CT and geometry descriptors from an existing artifact bundle without new calculations, strictly reusing the baseline bundle artifacts. The action registry includes two exact parse-only extraction actions that fit this need: `extract_ct_descriptors_from_bundle` for CT descriptors and `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` for geometry descriptors. Both support reuse-only mode and bounded extraction from an existing artifact bundle. Since the task requests both CT-descriptor and geometry-descriptor summaries, the best supported approach is to run both extraction actions sequentially or in parallel. However, the output contract allows only one execution action per microscopic decision. Given that, the best single action to cover the core CT-descriptor extraction (including charge redistribution and transition-localization clues) is `extract_ct_descriptors_from_bundle`, which directly supports CT localization proxies and charge distribution observables. For geometry descriptors, the `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` action is also available but cannot be combined in one execution action. Since the task explicitly requests both, but the microscopic worker can only select one action, the best fit is to select `extract_ct_descriptors_from_bundle` as the primary execution action (covering charge redistribution and CT proxies) and include `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` as a discovery or proxy action is not supported. Therefore, the decision is to execute `extract_ct_descriptors_from_bundle` with reuse_existing_artifacts_only=true, artifact_kind=baseline_bundle, artifact_bundle_id=round_01_baseline_bundle, and appropriate state_window and descriptor_scope parameters to cover CT descriptors relevant to ICT_vs_TICT discrimination. The geometry descriptor extraction is unsupported as a single action in this context. The task instruction forbids new calculations, so perform_new_calculation=false. This choice fulfills the main CT-descriptor extraction requirement exactly and partially covers the geometry-descriptor request by reporting explicit unresolved limits in the output. The residual unmet observable tags include geometry_descriptor. This is the best supported single-action translation given the registry and task constraints.
Capability limit note: The microscopic worker cannot execute multiple extraction actions simultaneously. The task requests both CT-descriptor and geometry-descriptor summaries, but only one execution action is allowed. The geometry-descriptor extraction action is available but cannot be combined with CT-descriptor extraction in one step. Therefore, geometry-descriptor extraction is not supported in this single-action decision and must be reported as an unmet observable. No new calculations are allowed, so only reuse of existing artifacts is permitted.
Failure policy: If the requested CT descriptors are not extractable from the existing baseline bundle artifacts, report unsupported parts explicitly. If geometry descriptors are not extractable in this single action, report them as residual unmet observables. Do not attempt new calculations or alternative actions beyond the registry. Return unsupported status only if CT-descriptor extraction is impossible.

raw_response.diagnosis: The task is to assess the likely AIE mechanism for a large neutral donor-acceptor aromatic molecule. From structure alone, the current top1 working hypothesis is ICT and the top2 runner-up is TICT. A further still-credible portfolio member is neutral aromatic, because the molecule has an extensive aromatic framework and a fairly high planarity/compactness proxy in the prepared context. ESIPT is currently weak due to the lack of an obvious proton-transfer motif. Top1 remains uncertain because the same architecture that supports donor-acceptor ICT also contains many peripheral aryl rotors that can support twist-enabled excited-state relaxation, and the large fused/aromatic content could still sustain substantial neutral-aromatic character. Shared prepared structure context is available and useful. Macro can only perform one bounded registry-backed structural screen and cannot make global mechanism decisions. Microscopic in round 1 can only perform one low-cost baseline S0/S1 action on the shared prepared structure and cannot do conformer sensitivity, torsion scans, or multi-step discrimination yet. Both macro and microscopic evidence should be gathered now because structural topology alone cannot separate ICT from TICT versus neutral aromatic, while baseline electronic-state evidence alone also cannot settle rotor-driven alternatives. Therefore the case should remain in portfolio screening rather than pairwise contraction in this initial round.
raw_response.task_instruction: Run one deterministic macro screen focused on donor-acceptor layout and one bounded baseline microscopic S0/S1 action on the shared prepared structure to gather indirect evidence that can reduce uncertainty across ICT, TICT, and neutral aromatic without prematurely collapsing the portfolio.

agent_task_instructions.Macro: Use exactly one registry-backed macro capability: screen_donor_acceptor_layout. Summarize donor-acceptor segmentation, conjugation continuity, and donor-acceptor partition cues from the provided molecule. Keep the report bounded and descriptive. Do not decide the global AIE mechanism. Frame the output as screening evidence relevant to ICT versus neutral aromatic, while noting that TICT cannot be excluded structurally by this single screen.
agent_task_instructions.Microscopic: Perform exactly one bounded baseline-only S0/S1 action on the shared prepared structure: a low-cost baseline route with shared-structure reuse, baseline S0 treatment, and bounded S1 vertical excitation. Report only direct observables available from that single action that bear on whether the lowest bright/accessible excitation appears charge-transfer-like or more locally excited/aromatic-like. Do not run conformer sensitivity, torsion sensitivity, follow-up optimizations, or make the final mechanism decision.

raw_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: ICT leads because the donor-acceptor architecture is strong, but that lead is provisional: the molecule also has substantial rotor content compatible with TICT and a large aromatic framework compatible with neutral-aromatic excitation. The most direct near-term screening evidence would be structural donor-acceptor layout cues plus a baseline excitation-character readout that can indicate whether charge separation is pronounced enough to keep ICT ahead of neutral aromatic, while leaving later torsion-sensitive work to test TICT more directly.
raw_response.capability_assessment: Macro can deliver only one deterministic, registry-backed structural screen this round; it cannot perform free-form multistep analysis or final mechanism adjudication. Microscopic can perform exactly one low-cost baseline S0/S1 action on the shared prepared structure and can provide only indirect first-round evidence about excitation character; it cannot in this round test conformer dependence, torsional relaxation, or multi-step excited-state workflows. This means TICT can be kept in the portfolio but not directly resolved in the first action pair.
